stem: You are the ED RMO managing a previously well 3-year-old girl presenting with 24 hours of fever and progressive lethargy. She has mottled skin, prolonged capillary refill, and cool peripheries. An IV line is in. The senior nurse is preparing fluids while you prepare to give broad-spectrum antibiotics within the first hour.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, BP 78/40, RR 42, SpO2 96% room air, T 39.6, capillary refill 5 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mottled cool peripheries, drowsy but rousable, no rash, chest clear, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.6 mmol/L, glucose 5.2, FBC and cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid strategy?

options.text: Administer a 10 to 20 mL/kg bolus of balanced crystalloid over 5 to 10 minutes with reassessment after each bolus
options.rationale: Current Australian and Surviving Sepsis paediatric guidance favours smaller boluses (10 to 20 mL/kg) with frequent reassessment to avoid fluid overload after the FEAST trial findings. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a 40 mL/kg bolus of normal saline over the first ten minutes prior to any reassessment of haemodynamics or perfusion markers
options.rationale: Large rapid boluses without reassessment risk pulmonary oedema and worse mortality in resource-rich settings as well; paediatric guidance is smaller incremental boluses. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis)

options.text: Start a peripheral noradrenaline infusion immediately as first-line haemodynamic support before any further crystalloid resuscitation is given
options.rationale: Vasopressors are second-line after initial crystalloid bolus and reassessment; immediate vasopressor in cold shock is not standard. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis)

options.text: Commence packed red blood cell transfusion as initial volume resuscitation while awaiting the full blood count and group and screen results
options.rationale: Blood is not first-line volume; crystalloid is unless there is documented haemorrhage or low haemoglobin requiring transfusion. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis)

options.text: Administer 5% albumin 20 mL/kg as the preferred crystalloid alternative for septic shock fluid resuscitation in paediatric practice
options.rationale: Albumin is not preferred first-line; balanced crystalloid is recommended and albumin reserved for refractory shock if used at all. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis)

explanation.summary: Paediatric sepsis fluid resuscitation in Australia uses 10 to 20 mL/kg boluses of balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte 148 or Hartmann's preferred over normal saline) with reassessment after each bolus. The FEAST trial showed harm from aggressive fluid boluses in African children with sepsis and informs cautious incremental dosing. Antibiotics within 1 hour and vasoactive support if perfusion does not improve after 40 to 60 mL/kg total are the next steps.
key_points: 10 to 20 mL/kg bolus then reassess, not 40 to 60 mL/kg blindly
Balanced crystalloid preferred over normal saline
Antibiotics within the first hour; vasopressors after 40 to 60 mL/kg if not responding
explanation.pearls: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2020 paediatric update recommends starting vasopressors via peripheral access if not responding after 40 to 60 mL/kg crystalloid rather than delaying for central access.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 12-year-old girl in clinic after a third episode of syncope while swimming in cold water at school carnivals. Each episode involved sudden loss of consciousness with rapid recovery. Her ECG at rest shows a QTc of 488 ms calculated using the Bazett formula. Her paternal grandfather died suddenly aged 32 of unknown cause while swimming.

data_table.Examination: Normal cardiovascular and neurological examination, no dysmorphism
data_table.Investigations: Resting ECG QTc 488 ms (Bazett), no arrhythmia on Holter; electrolytes including magnesium normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Commence flecainide as antiarrhythmic prophylaxis pending genetic confirmation while continuing routine sport participation as tolerated by the patient
options.rationale: Flecainide is not first-line for LQTS and can prolong QT further; sport restriction is part of LQT1 management until confirmed. (Source: HRS Inherited Arrhythmia Statement)

options.text: Commence metoprolol succinate at a paediatric dose and reassess in three months with repeat resting ECG to evaluate the effect on QTc and clinical symptoms
options.rationale: Selective beta1 blockers like metoprolol are inferior to nadolol and propranolol in LQTS and not first-line for paediatric patients with high-risk features. (Source: HRS Inherited Arrhythmia Statement)

options.text: Implant a single-chamber ICD as primary prevention given the family history and three syncopal episodes despite a normal Holter recording
options.rationale: Beta-blockers and lifestyle changes are first-line; ICD is reserved for breakthrough events on therapy or aborted cardiac arrest, not initial management. (Source: HRS Inherited Arrhythmia Statement)

options.text: Commence a non-selective beta-blocker such as nadolol or propranolol with referral for genetic testing and family cascade screening
options.rationale: Suspected LQT1 with swimming-triggered syncope and prolonged QTc plus family history requires beta-blockade (nadolol or propranolol preferred) and genetic confirmation with family screening. (Source: HRS Inherited Arrhythmia Statement)

options.text: Reassure the family and arrange a tilt-table test to confirm vasovagal syncope as the likely diagnosis based on the recurrent self-limiting episodes
options.rationale: Exertion or swimming-triggered syncope with prolonged QTc and family sudden death is not vasovagal; further LQTS evaluation is required. (Source: HRS Inherited Arrhythmia Statement)

explanation.summary: Swimming-triggered syncope is the signature presentation of LQT1, which accounts for around 30 to 35% of LQTS. A QTc above 480 ms in a symptomatic patient plus family sudden death is diagnostic by Schwartz score. Non-selective beta-blockers (nadolol or propranolol) are first-line; selective agents are inferior. ICD is reserved for breakthrough events or aborted cardiac arrest. Genetic testing guides family cascade screening.
key_points: LQT1 triggers: exercise and swimming; LQT2: auditory stimuli, postpartum; LQT3: rest and sleep
Nadolol and propranolol superior to selective beta-blockers in LQTS
ICD for aborted cardiac arrest or recurrent syncope on optimal beta-blockade
explanation.pearls: QTc above 480 ms with unexplained syncope scores 3 on Schwartz criteria; combined with family sudden death under 30, this triggers LQTS workup even without genetic confirmation.

sources.label: CSANZ Genetic Heart Disease Position Statement

stem: You are the PICU registrar receiving a 6-day-old breastfed boy retrieved from a regional centre with progressive lethargy and refusal to feed since day 4. He has had two brief tonic-clonic events. The infant is now obtunded. Bedside glucose is 4.2 mmol/L. The retrieval team has sent off a metabolic screen and ammonia.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 188, RR 70, T 36.4, SpO2 98% on minimal oxygen
data_table.Examination: Obtunded, hypotonic, no focal neurology, no dysmorphic features, normal newborn examination at birth
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 4.2, lactate 1.8, venous pH 7.49, bicarbonate 22, ammonia 540 micromol/L, anion gap 14, urine ketones negative
data_table.Medications: Nil; standard newborn vitamin K and hepatitis B at birth

lead_in: Which finding most strongly directs initial management?

options.text: Hypoglycaemia requiring high-rate dextrose infusion at 8 mg/kg/min with concurrent insulin to facilitate intracellular potassium shift
options.rationale: Glucose is 4.2 mmol/L, not low; insulin would be inappropriate and dangerous in this context. (Source: ANZSIEM Hyperammonaemia Guideline)

options.text: Hyperammonaemia with respiratory alkalosis and absent ketosis directing urgent referral for haemofiltration and a urea cycle defect pathway
options.rationale: Marked hyperammonaemia (over 200 micromol/L) with respiratory alkalosis and absent ketosis in a previously well neonate after milk feeds points to a urea cycle defect; haemofiltration is the definitive ammonia-lowering treatment alongside protein restriction and ammonia scavengers. (Source: ANZSIEM Hyperammonaemia Guideline)

options.text: Metabolic acidosis with raised anion gap and ketosis pointing toward organic acidaemia requiring sodium bicarbonate and biotin empirically
options.rationale: Anion gap is 14 and pH is alkalotic with absent ketones, which excludes organic acidaemia as the primary problem. (Source: ANZSIEM Hyperammonaemia Guideline)

options.text: Raised lactate suggesting mitochondrial disease and requiring high-dose thiamine, riboflavin, and coenzyme Q10 prior to further imaging
options.rationale: Lactate is 1.8 mmol/L which is within normal limits and does not support mitochondrial disease as the primary diagnosis. (Source: ANZSIEM Hyperammonaemia Guideline)

options.text: Normal newborn examination history excluding inborn error of metabolism and directing toward neonatal sepsis pathway and empiric antibiotics
options.rationale: Many inborn errors present after milk feeds are established with a symptom-free interval after birth; normal initial examination does not exclude them. (Source: ANZSIEM Hyperammonaemia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Symptom-free interval followed by lethargy and encephalopathy after milk feeds in a neonate signals an inborn error of metabolism. Urea cycle defects classically cause hyperammonaemia with respiratory alkalosis and absent ketosis; organic acidaemias cause metabolic acidosis with raised anion gap and ketosis. Ammonia above 200 in a neonate is a haemofiltration indication. Stop protein, give 10% dextrose to suppress catabolism, and give ammonia scavengers (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate, arginine).
key_points: Symptom-free interval after birth then encephalopathy with milk feeds suggests urea cycle defect
Urea cycle defect: respiratory alkalosis, absent ketosis; organic acidaemia: metabolic acidosis with ketosis
Ammonia over 200 micromol/L in a neonate is a haemofiltration trigger
explanation.pearls: Boys with X-linked ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency typically present in the first week with this picture; carrier mothers may have a history of protein aversion or postpartum psychiatric symptoms.

stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 32-year-old G2P1 woman at 26 weeks gestation with a BMI of 31. Her first child was 4.3 kg at term with no documented dysglycaemia in pregnancy. She has just completed a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test in the morning after appropriate fasting and brings the printout to the appointment.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height appropriate for dates, fetal movements present
data_table.Investigations: OGTT: fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 1 hour 9.8 mmol/L, 2 hour 7.9 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Order an HbA1c level to differentiate between gestational diabetes and overt pregestational diabetes that may have been missed at initial booking visit
options.rationale: HbA1c is useful at booking to detect overt diabetes but is not the diagnostic test at 26 weeks; OGTT thresholds apply. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the patient as none of the OGTT values meet the diagnostic threshold for gestational diabetes and continue routine antenatal care without changes
options.rationale: The fasting value of 5.4 exceeds the 5.1 mmol/L threshold and the 2 hour exceeds 8.5; the test is positive. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Commence metformin immediately as first-line pharmacological therapy without a trial of dietary intervention given the elevated body mass index
options.rationale: First-line is diet and exercise with capillary monitoring; pharmacotherapy follows if targets are not met. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the OGTT in two weeks to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention or referral as a single test may produce false positive results
options.rationale: GDM is diagnosed on a single OGTT meeting ADIPS thresholds; repeating delays care. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes and commence dietary advice with capillary glucose monitoring four times daily and dietitian referral
options.rationale: ADIPS thresholds (fasting 5.1, 1h 10.0, 2h 8.5) are met by fasting and 2 hour values; diagnosis of GDM follows with diet first and monitoring. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

explanation.summary: ADIPS criteria for GDM diagnosis on 75 g OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks: fasting at least 5.1, 1 hour at least 10.0, 2 hour at least 8.5 mmol/L. Any single elevated value diagnoses GDM. First-line management is medical nutrition therapy plus daily monitoring (fasting target under 5.0 and 2 hour postprandial under 6.7). Metformin or insulin is added if targets are not met after 1 to 2 weeks of lifestyle change.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 mmol/L are the AU diagnostic criteria
Diet and monitoring first; pharmacotherapy if targets not reached in 1 to 2 weeks
Insulin is first-line pharmacotherapy in AU; metformin is acceptable alternative
explanation.pearls: ADIPS recommends repeat OGTT 6 to 12 weeks postpartum and lifetime annual diabetes screening because lifetime T2DM risk is around 50% after GDM.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar called urgently to the birthing suite for a multiparous woman ten minutes after a spontaneous vaginal birth with active third stage management. The placenta is out and appears complete. The midwife reports estimated blood loss of 900 mL with continuing brisk bleeding. The uterus is palpably soft and rises above the umbilicus on examination.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/58, HR 116, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy at 24 weeks size, no perineal lacerations, bladder palpable
data_table.Investigations: Group and screen on file, FBC and coags being sent
data_table.Medications: IV oxytocin 10 units given at delivery; nil drug allergies; BP 144/92 on antenatal booking with no other features

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next action?

options.text: Transfer the patient to the operating theatre for examination under anaesthesia and exploration without further attempts at conservative bedside management
options.rationale: Theatre is appropriate if first-line medical and mechanical measures fail; immediate transfer skips simpler effective steps. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

options.text: Administer carboprost 250 micrograms intramuscularly as the first additional uterotonic without bimanual compression or further bladder management
options.rationale: Carboprost is third-line after oxytocin and ergometrine; mechanical measures and second uterotonic come first. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

options.text: Empty the bladder, perform bimanual compression, and administer a further uterotonic such as syntometrine while calling for senior assistance
options.rationale: Uterine atony with full bladder is the leading cause of PPH; bladder emptying, bimanual compression, and additional uterotonic combined with senior call form the immediate response. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

options.text: Administer tranexamic acid one gram intravenously as the sole pharmacological response to ongoing postpartum bleeding from a soft uterus
options.rationale: TXA is given alongside uterotonics, not in place of them; sole TXA without addressing atony is insufficient. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

options.text: Reassure the team that 900 mL is within acceptable physiological blood loss after vaginal birth and continue observation without intervention
options.rationale: PPH is defined as 500 mL or more after vaginal birth; 900 mL with ongoing bleeding requires escalation. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement)

explanation.summary: PPH management follows the 4Ts framework: tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin. Tone (atony) is by far the most common cause. Initial actions include bladder emptying, uterine massage and bimanual compression, additional uterotonics (oxytocin infusion, ergometrine if BP allows, syntometrine, carboprost, misoprostol), TXA 1 g IV, fluid resuscitation, and senior call. Persistent bleeding triggers theatre for EUA, balloon tamponade, and surgical control.
key_points: Bladder emptying is a high-yield often-missed first step
Uterotonic sequence: oxytocin then ergometrine (avoid in HTN) then carboprost or misoprostol
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces mortality (WOMAN trial)
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG PPH bundle: massage, empty bladder, second uterotonic, TXA, IV access x2, group and crossmatch, senior call, theatre activation if not responding.
